sources.notes: Construct a permutation minimizing the mex of the set of mex values over given intervals.
Official editorial proves mex(M)<=2 via subsequence [0,2,1], then handles the cases where mex(M)=0 or mex(M)=1 is possible.
Additional solution note matches the three-case construction from the official editorial.

annotation.summary: Construct a permutation of 0..n-1 that minimizes the mex of all interval mex values.
annotation.constraints: n,m are large enough that the construction must be based on interval boundary properties, not search over permutations.
annotation.core_idea: Putting [0,2,1] as a subsequence ensures any interval containing both 0 and 1 also contains 2, so mex(M) is at most 2. To get mex(M)=0, all intervals must share a common point where 0 can be placed. To get mex(M)=1, place 0 next to 1 at a point that is not simultaneously an interval end and the next interval start, ensuring every interval containing 0 also contains 1. If neither condition holds, use the [0,2,1] fallback.

solution_variants.name: three-case-permutation-construction
solution_variants.summary: Try a common-point construction for mex 0, then adjacent 0/1 for mex 1, otherwise place 0,2,1 as a subsequence.

tags.evidence: The problem is solved by explicitly placing the values 0,1,2 according to interval boundary cases.
The solution separates mex(M)=0, mex(M)=1, and fallback mex(M)=2 constructions.
The algorithm explicitly detects common intersection and forbidden boundary positions before falling back.
